content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662        322-5660              322-5901          322-6441

July 2, 1985


Beatrice Cooley
Kevin Wolf
Friends of the River
909 - 12th Street, Suite 207
Sacramento, CA. 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice on
Reporting Requirements for
Legislator/VIP River Raft
Trips
Our File No. A-85-125

Dear Ms. Cooley and Mr. Wolf:

Thank you for your request for advice from this office. We
provided related advice to you in 1983; a copy of that letter is
enclosed (A-83-160).

My understanding of the situation is as follows. Friends
of the River is planning to offer rafting trips this summer on
Wild and Scenic and endangered rivers to a number of
legislators, their families and staff. Generally there are two
types of trips. The first is where Friends of the River arrange
for a trip for these persons through a commercial outfitter.
These trips are normally sold for $160 per person; however, the
outfitter is able to reduce the price to $110 per these trips
because certain costs are reduced or eliminated, and the
outfitter is willing to minimize his profits. The second type
of trip is arranged directly by your organization. Members
donate boats; and river guides volunteer their time. The cost
to each person on the trip is $85.

If the commercial outfitter provides the trip for $110 and
the legislator is given a free trip, the legislator is required
to report on his or her statement of economic interests a gift
valued at $110 from the outfitter.1/ If each member of the

1/ Generally the value of a gift is its fair market
value. To the extent that the $110 figure reflects a discount
or minimization of profits by the outfitter given specially to
the legislators, the reported value should be more than $110.

Beatrice Cooley
Kevin Wolf
July 2, 1985


legislator's family or staff pays $110 for the trip, it is not a
reportable transaction. However, if the legislator and spouse
are both given free trips, the answer depends on whether the
legislator is given control over who receives the trips. If the
free trips are expressly given to "Legislator X and Mr. (or
Mrs.) X," then the gifts are considered to be separate gifts to
the legislator and the spouse. But if two trips are given to
"Legislator X and his or her guest," and the legislator thus
exercises direction or control over who goes on the trips, then
both trips must be reported by the legislator as a gift with a
value of $220.

If the Friends of the River provide the river trips for
$85, again, the answers for the three situations involving the
gift to the legislator, the payment for the trip by staff or
family, and a gift to the legislator and his or her spouse are
exactly the same as in the commercial outfitter situation.2/
The only difference is the source of the gifts.

Please note that the lobbyist for Friends of the River must
not arrange the trip or make contact in any manner with any
legislator regarding the trip. Section 86203. The lobbyist may
go on the trip as long as she or he is not the sole representa-
tive of Friends of the River. See Opinion requested by
Institute of Governmental Advocates, 7 FPPC Opinions 1 (No.
81-003, Feb. 1, 1982).

You also inquired about the potential for disqualification
by a legislator who receives gifts from Friends of the River
which are valued at $250 or more. Under Section 87103, a
legislator will not be required to disqualify him or herself
from participation in a decision unless the decision will have a
material financial effect on the Friends of the River. Since
Friends of the River is a nonprofit grass-roots organization
that appears to draw no pecuniary benefits from the legislation
it actively supports or opposes, it is unlikely that
disqualification would ever be required.

I hope this has answered your questions as to your
VIP/Legislator River Raft Program. Please feel free to contact

2/ It appears that, in this situation, $85 is a fair
estimate of the market value of the trips.

Beatrice Cooley
Kevin Wolf
July 2, 1985


this office again if you have any additional questions or
concerns.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Diane Maura/Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMP:plh

FRIENDS
OF THE
RIVER

[illegible]

May 20, 1985


Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Sir or Madam:

     We are requesting clarification of the reporting requirements for our
Legislator/VIP river trips this summer. We plan to introduce a number of
legislators, their families and staff people to the values of free-flowing
rivers by offering rafting trips on Wild and Scenic and endangered rivers.
The two most common types of trips are outlined below. We would appreciate
your verification of the necessity for reporting which would result from each
one.

     1. We arrange for one or more Legislators, members of their staffs and
families to participate in a river trip provided by a commercial outfitter.
The outfitter advertises the trip in the company brochure for $160 per
person. The outfitter is able to reduce the cost of the trip because
     - the guides are willing to volunteer their time,
     - the shuttle cost is decreased by arranging for a volunteer to row
baggage instead of paying a driver to haul it in on the road and
     - food costs are minimized by providing less extravagant meals than
usual.

     The outfitter is also willing to minimize his profit for such a trip
and makes the trip available to each person on the trip (guides excepted) for
$110.
     We would consider each of the following to represent correct reporting
of this trip:
     a. The Legislator is given a free trip and is required to report a gift
value of $110.
     b. Each member of the Legislator's family or staff pays $110 and is
therefore not required to report a gift.
     c. The Legislator and spouse are both given free trips, resulting in a
report of $220.


909 12th Street, Suite 207, Sacramento, CA 95814 (916) 442-3155 • S.F. Office: Fort Mason Center, Building C., San Francisco, CA 94123 (415) 771-0400

2. Friends of the River provides river trips to the same people on
these same rivers. We use boats donated for the purpose by our members,
provide guides who volunteer their time and share the expenses of shuttle,
food and equipment transportation. The cost to each person on the trip
(guides excepted) is $85.

a. The Legislator is given a free trip and is required to report a gift
value of $85.
b. Each member of the Legislator's family or staff pays $85 and is
therefore not required to report a gift.
c. The Legislator and spouse are both given free trips, resulting in a
report of $170.

We have made several assumptions. One is that each gift of a trip to a
staff person or family member is reportable as a gift to the legislator.
Another is that as long as the total value of gifts remains under $250, a
legislator is under no requirement to be disqualified on river issues.
Should disqualification result however, what is the term of the disqual-
ification?

Thank you very much for responding to our questions. We appreciate the
time and effort required to assist us in planning our VIP trips.

Sincerely yours,

Beatrice Cooley                    Kevin Wolf
Special Projects Coordinator      River Programs Coordinator

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

These trips are normally sold for $160 per person; however, the
outfitter is able to reduce the price to $110 for these trips
because certain costs are reduced or eliminated, and the
outfitter is willing to minimize his profits.  The second type
of trip is arranged directly by your organization.  Members
donate boats; and river guides volunteer their time.  The cost
to each person on the trip is $85.

     If the commercial outfitter provides the trip for $110 and
the legislator is given a free trip, the legislator is required
to report on his or her statement of economic interests a gift
valued at $110 from the outfitter.1/  If each member of the

_________________________
1/  Generally the value of a gift is its fair market
value.  To the extent that the $110 figure reflects a discount
or minimization of profits by the outfitter given specially to
the legislators, the reported value should be more than $110.

Beatrice Cooley
Kevin Wolf
July 2, 1985


legislator's family or staff pays $110 for the trip, it is not a
reportable transaction. However, if the legislator and spouse
are both given free trips, the answer depends on whether the
legislator is given control over who receives the trips. If the
free trips are expressly given to "legislator X and Mr. (or
Mrs.)", the gifts are considered to be separate gifts to
the legislator and the spouse. But if two trips are given to
"legislator X and his or her guest," and the legislator thus
exercises direction or control over who goes on the trips, then
both trips must be reported by the legislator as a gift with a
value of $220.

If the Friends of the River provide the river trips for
$85, again, the answers for the three situations involving the

exactly the same as in the commercial outfitter situation.2/
The only difference is in the source of the gifts.

Please note that the lobbyist for Friends of the River must
not arrange the trip or make contact in any manner with any
legislator regarding the trip. Section 86203. The lobbyist may
go on the trip as long as she or he is not the sole representa-
tive of Friends of the River. See Opinion requested by
Institute of Governmental Advocates, 7 FPPC Opinions 1 (No.
81-003, Feb. 1, 1982).

You also inquired about the potential for disqualification
by a legislator who receives gifts from Friends of the River
which are valued at $250 or more. Under Section 87103, a
legislator will not be required to disqualify him or herself
from participation in a decision unless the decision will have a
material financial effect on the Friends of the River. Since
Friends of the River is a nonprofit grass-roots organization
that appears to draw no pecuniary benefits from the legislation
it actively supports or opposes, it is unlikely that
disqualification would ever be required.

I hope this has answered your questions as to your
VIP/Legislator River Raft Program. Please feel free to contact

2/ It appears that, in this situation, $85 is a fair
estimate of the market value of the trips.

Beatrice Cooley
Kevin Wolf
July 2, 1985


this office again if you have any additional questions or
concerns.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura/Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

sections.question: We are requesting clarification of the reporting requirements for our Legislator/VIP river trips this summer. We plan to introduce a number of legislators, their families and staff people to the values of free-flowing rivers by offering rafting trips on Wild and Scenic and endangered rivers. The two most common types of trips are outlined below. We would appreciate your verification of the necessity for reporting which would result from each one. [Details on commercial outfitter trips at $110 per person and Friends of the River trips at $85 per person, with scenarios involving free trips for legislators, paid trips for staff/family, and trips for legislators and spouses. Also inquired about disqualification requirements when gifts exceed $250.]
sections.conclusion: If the commercial outfitter provides the trip for $110 and the legislator is given a free trip, the legislator is required to report on his or her statement of economic interests a gift valued at $110 from the outfitter. If each member of the legislator's family or staff pays $110 for the trip, it is not a reportable transaction. If the legislator and spouse are both given free trips and the legislator exercises direction or control over who goes on the trips, both trips must be reported by the legislator as a gift with a value of $220. If Friends of the River provide river trips for $85, the answers are exactly the same as in the commercial outfitter situation. The lobbyist for Friends of the River must not arrange the trip or make contact with any legislator regarding the trip, though the lobbyist may go on the trip if not the sole representative. Under Section 87103, a legislator will not be required to disqualify themselves unless the decision will have a material financial effect on the Friends of the River, which is unlikely since it is a nonprofit grassroots organization that draws no pecuniary benefits from the legislation it supports or opposes.
sections.question_synthetic: Friends of the River sought clarification on reporting requirements for legislators receiving free or discounted rafting trips, either through commercial outfitters or directly organized, and whether disqualification from voting is required when gifts exceed $250.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advised that free trips valued at $110 or $85 must be reported as gifts by legislators, that paying family/staff members are not reportable, that control over trip allocation determines whether dual trips are reported as one or two gifts, that the organization's lobbyist cannot solicit legislators, and that disqualification is unlikely since the nonprofit organization receives no pecuniary benefits from legislation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (dated May 20, 1985) and FPPC response letter (dated July 2, 1985). Extracted information focuses on the FPPC response letter as the primary advice letter. The question text was reconstructed from the incoming request section since the response letter references rather than verbatim restates the question. Minor footnotes addressing fair market value and cost estimation are included in the conclusion extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: We are requesting clarification of the reporting requirements for our Legislator/VIP river trips this summer. We plan to introduce a number of legislators, their families and staff people to the values of free-flowing rivers by offering rafting trips on Wild and Scenic and endangered rivers. The two most common types of trips are outlined below. We would appreciate your verification of the necessity for reporting which would result from each one. [Details on commercial outfitter trips at $110 per person and Friends of the River trips at $85 per person, with scenarios involving free trips for legislators, paid trips for staff/family, and trips for legislators and spouses. Also inquired about disqualification requirements when gifts exceed $250.]

CONCLUSION: If the commercial outfitter provides the trip for $110 and the legislator is given a free trip, the legislator is required to report on his or her statement of economic interests a gift valued at $110 from the outfitter. If each member of the legislator's family or staff pays $110 for the trip, it is not a reportable transaction. If the legislator and spouse are both given free trips and the legislator exercises direction or control over who goes on the trips, both trips must be reported by the legislator as a gift with a value of $220. If Friends of the River provide river trips for $85, the answers are exactly the same as in the commercial outfitter situation. The lobbyist for Friends of the River must not arrange the trip or make contact with any legislator regarding the trip, though the lobbyist may go on the trip if not the sole representative. Under Section 87103, a legislator will not be required to disqualify themselves unless the decision will have a material financial effect on the Friends of the River, which is unlikely since it is a nonprofit grassroots organization that draws no pecuniary benefits from the legislation it supports or opposes.

embedding.summary: FPPC provided guidance to Friends of the River on reporting requirements for free or discounted river rafting trips offered to California legislators, their families, and staff, addressing gift valuation, reporting obligations, lobbyist restrictions, and disqualification provisions. The advice applied the same reporting rules regardless of whether trips were arranged through commercial outfitters or directly by the organization.
